speaker: interviewer
text: --is being interviewed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- a picture of my grandfather, Richard Schaeffer, who lived in Hamburg with his wife, Agnes, my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about when this was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a drawing done by a relative of his. And it is very lifelike. He was a tall, stately gentleman, much respected and loved by all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was your mother able to ship this out when she came from Germany? Or did she get this later?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she got this later, from-- from the relative who did it. And she gave it to her later. He-- he died of natural-- of old age in-- in London. And I had tea with him every afternoon. OK. These are my parents on the day they got engaged. Kate Schaeffer to Oscar Loeser.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? About?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1923, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg. This is Lotte Elkon, my mother's young sister, who is the person who saved all our lives. My family's, and my grandparents', my uncles and aunts and their children. Without her, we would probably not be alive anymore today. This was on her own wedding. Unfortunately, she did not a happy-- have a happy life. I was very close to her. And I believe it was-- she was fond of me, too. This is the entrance to the apartment building we lived in at number 8 [GERMAN] in Eppendorf, in Hamburg. We lived on the second floor. And I moved here with my parents when I was two years old and left when I was 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In 1970-- almost 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this was when you returned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I returned with both my children, to show them where I used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there had never been any bomb damage here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No bomb damage to this. And we went inside into our apartment, my apartment. And it was-- had turned into two apartments by that time. Two families lived there, where before only one family had lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- were they happy to show you around? Or-- or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were very moved by our visit, as a matter of fact. A Dutch family lived there. This is the balcony where we used to-- my mother used to grow plants. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not a Jewish family who was living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not a Jewish family who lived there now. I only met one part of the families. This is the window to the dining room, it used to be a dining room. And the-- up on top, the upper left is a living room window. But it was on the balcony where we sometimes ate supper or on a hot day or we children played. We had no garden, being a apartment house. The-- yeah. OK. OK. Just come back to it and see how different it looked. And yet, it was the same. The same rooms were just petitioned off or made into a different kind of layout than before. And the people were very gracious and let us look around.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're an--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a class, my class in public school with my teacher, Puck. And I am standing next to [NON-ENGLISH], who was another Jewish child, and whose mother was a very great, very famous singer at the time. At this time, this was the first time I began to realize that things were different, due to the-- some of the boys in my class having had to join the Hitler Youth and wearing the much-hated Hitler uniform, and being absent from school because they had to serve under the-- in the Hitler Youth. It's a brown uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many of these children survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, no. We did not keep up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of any of these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm afraid not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it take you back to your childhood, experiencing the feelings that you had, seeing those little--

speaker: interviewee
text: It certainly does, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Oswald, my only sibling. He's five years younger than I am. And this was taken in England, shortly after we'd arrived in England from Hamburg on the Kindertransport. At 9 years old. This is brother Oswald again, a few years later. And he's well on his way in becoming an actor with the British Shakespeare Company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he make that a career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Partially, yes. He dropped it after a while and turned to something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The following photograph is taken from this book. This book has stories from people from Hamburg about incidents in-- during the Nazi time--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you just translate the title for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The title is Fremd in der Eigenen Stadt. Means being a foreigner in your own city. Or being a stranger in your own city.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the children on the children's transport as we were landing in England and leaving the ship. There am I, in the middle of a crowd, somehow facing my brother, who is leaning against the wall, just showing his [INAUDIBLE], part of his [INAUDIBLE]. At this time, I am still a member-- I am a member of the Kindertransport Association. And-- which is an association which was founded a few years ago. They have a monthly or bi-monthly magazine they bring out. And-- and they have regular meetings and gatherings of people who have been on the Kindertransport-- in the Kindertransport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- have you met other people through this organization who-- who traveled with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have never met anyone who was on the same transport, yet. But of course, everyone whom I've met there has been on some transport or other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there some similarity in their story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Except--

speaker: interviewer
text: --theme? What is that theme?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all left our parents behind and didn't know whether we would ever see them again. This picture is of Andrew and myself, just shortly before we got married in London. in 1948. It was taken by a colleague of Andrew's, who worked with him in the office in London, in the insurance office at [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you people-- you speak German or English to one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke English with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which we kept-- wisely or not so wisely when our children were growing up, it was too hard for us to-- to speak in German to them somehow. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was too hard emotionally to speak to them in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. The only time we spoke German-- and, again, that wasn't very wise-- was when we didn't want them to understand something. And we had both said we would never do this. Because our parents spoke French so we couldn't understand in Germany. And we'd always said we wouldn't do it. And yet, we did do it. And children did not appreciate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture painted, myself, when I was five years old, just shortly before my brother was born. The painter was called Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]. And he painted two pictures of me. The main one, which he sold to my parents, was in a blue dress. I imagine matching my eyes. And he made it-- because he liked it so much, he made a second one, in a brown dress. Which he did-- we did not know about. The original one, in the blue dress, was burned in [NON-ENGLISH], in Hamburg, in-- when Hamburg's ports were bombed. And-- and none of our belongings survived that. And I came across this picture one evening, when I was attending a meeting in London, during the war. And I-- this meeting took place at somebody's house who-- it was a stranger. And as I walked into the living room, the first thing I was greeted by was myself hanging over the mantelpiece in the brown dress. It was a tremendous experience, tremendous shock to me. And at first, I wasn't sure that it was really my picture. I told my parents about it. And they got in touch with the owners of the picture and bought it from them. And it's been in our possession ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ask, or did anybody say, how they got-- how they got this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were family of the painter, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and that's how-- how it remained there. That he had given it to them or lent it to them when he emigrated. I'm not sure exactly what the story is. They were reluctant to give it up. But they understood. OK. This is my husband, Andrew. We were happily married for almost 30 years. And this was a few years before he died of cancer in 1978. This was taken in his office, where he was very, very happy. And it's too bad he isn't here today. OK. OK. This is the picture taken in-- on Saint John's island in the Virgin Islands, where our entire family spent a week in a gorgeous house, having a wonderful time. Starting from left to right, in the front row is Timothy Rossmere, sitting on his father's lap, Eve, his sister, Eve Rossmere, sitting on my lap, and Elena Perry, sitting on my daughter's lap. Second row is my son, David, myself, and Jennifer, my daughter. And the last row, left to right, is [? Ronnie ?] Rossmere, my-- David's wife, and [INAUDIBLE] Perry, Jennifer's husband. And father of little Elena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you want to say to your children here? I know they're unable to-- to be here physically today. Is there any message that you want to give them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I love them very much. Wish they could be here too. This little guy is Andrew, called Drew by his parents. He's not a year old. As a matter of fact, he's nine months old and just learning to stand and walk by himself. He lives with his parents and sister in Maryland, in a lovely house they moved into recently.
